title: Artificial human companion
body:
An artificial human companion is a device or application designed to simulate companionship through social, emotional, or relational interaction. Examples of these systems include conversational agents, chatbots, digital pets, virtual avatars, or physically embodied robots. 
Unlike task-oriented digital assistants, artificial human companions' capabilities include not just practical support but also often provide emotional presence, maintain ongoing social engagement, and/or cultivate interactions that resemble interpersonal relationships. They can engage in natural and dynamic conversations, provide assistance, offer companionship, and even perform tasks like scheduling or information retrieval. Their capabilities have expanded significantly with advances in artificial intelligence, large language models (LLMs), affective computing, and social robotics.
Astrosynthetic intimacy is a proposed conceptual framework describing emotionally significant interactions between humans and artificial intelligence systems in extraterrestrial and long-duration spaceflight environments. The concept was introduced by Nigerian-Canadian artificial intelligence researcher and academic Muyideen Dele Adewale in his 2026 study on AI-mediated companionship and psychosocial wellbeing in space missions.
The framework examines the potential emergence of emotional attachment, companionship, and relational dependency between astronauts and AI-driven synthetic companions during prolonged missions to environments such as the Moon and Mars. It draws from research in human–computer interaction, affective computing, social robotics, digital intimacy, and space psychology.
Astrosynthetic intimacy was proposed within the context of increasing scholarly interest in the psychosocial risks associated with isolation, confinement, and communication delays in long-duration spaceflight. Adewale argued that emotionally adaptive AI systems may function as psychological support mechanisms, social stabilisers, and behavioural regulation tools in extraterrestrial habitats.
The concept is associated with the proposed Modular Autonomous Intimacy Companion Architecture (MAICA), a speculative framework for AI-mediated emotional support systems designed for future space missions. The framework also discusses ethical concerns surrounding emotional dependency, privacy, autonomy, manipulation, and governance of emotionally responsive AI systems in closed environments.
Astrosynthetic intimacy has been discussed as part of broader debates surrounding synthetic relationships, AI companionship, and the societal implications of emotionally intelligent artificial systems.
